name: getOrchestrator
description:
Starts a synchronous orchestrator for complex multi-step tasks. The orchestrator runs in its own isolated AI context and has access to the configured specialist execution tool (default: getSpecialists) to launch parallel specialist workers. Blocks until the orchestrator has fully completed and returns its final result.

USE getOrchestrator when the task has ANY of these characteristics:
- Multi-step: requires multiple sequential or parallel tool calls to complete
- High complexity: needs decomposition into specialized sub-tasks
- High parallelizability: contains multiple independent sub-tasks that benefit from concurrent execution
- History/context retrieval beyond the visible context window
- A direct tool call (not via getOrchestrator) returned an error or failure: immediately delegate the full original task to the orchestrator instead of retrying the failed tool directly or aborting the turn

Modes:
- mode="plan": DEFAULT. Returns only a concrete execution plan for user review. Use this when no explicit mode is required by the calling context.
- mode="execute": Executes immediately. Only use when explicitly instructed to do so by the system prompt or user.
policyHint: Follow the system prompt's instructions for which mode to use. If the system prompt says to use mode="execute", use it immediately without showing a plan. If no instruction is given, use mode="plan" first and show the plan before executing.

prompt.description: The task or goal for the orchestrator to accomplish. Be specific and include all relevant context.

mode.description: Execution mode. 'plan' returns only a plan for approval. 'execute' runs immediately when the calling channel or system prompt explicitly requires execution. In approval-based channels, pass approvedPlan when executing an approved plan.

approvedPlan.description: The exact plan text previously shown to and approved by the user. Required when mode='execute'.

type.description: The orchestrator type to use. This is a configured type name such as 'context', 'generic', or a channel-specific type like 'dnd-file'. If omitted, the configured defaultType is used.
